items.myth: Missing values should always be filled with the column mean.
items.reality: Mean imputation is a quick baseline but pulls tails toward the center, ignores mechanism (MAR/MNAR), and is often worse than median, group-wise, or model-based fills.
items.example: Mean-filling income in a right-skewed column replaces high earners with a typical value, flattening separation the classifier needed.

items.myth: If you delete all rows with missing data, you remove bias.
items.reality: Listwise deletion is unbiased mainly under MCAR; when missingness depends on observed or hidden values, complete-case samples are not representative of the population.
items.example: Dropping respondents who skipped an income question leaves a wealthier-looking sample because low earners opted out—MNAR bias.

items.myth: Tree models make missing-value preprocessing unnecessary.
items.reality: Gradient-boosted trees handle NaN in their own splits, but mixed pipelines still need imputation for linear heads, neural nets, and distance-based steps on the same features.
items.example: A LightGBM plus logistic calibration stack still needs finite inputs for the logistic layer even if trees saw raw NaN.
